ontology.foodex2_description: Shrimps and prawns — cooked by frying
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 code A0CKM covers shrimps and prawns cooked by frying; no specific amaebi karaage code exists. Used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard appetizer serving of amaebi karaage is approximately 150 g, typically comprising 6–8 whole deep-fried sweet shrimp served with a dipping sauce or lemon wedge.

nutrient_source.title: USDA FoodData Central — Shrimp, mixed species, cooked, breaded and fried (FDC ID 175180)
nutrient_source.organization: U.S. Department of Agriculture, Agricultural Research Service
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No USDA FoodData Central entry exists specifically for amaebi (sweet shrimp / Pandalus borealis) karaage. Values are adapted from USDA FDC entry for breaded and fried mixed-species shrimp (FDC ID 175180), which is the closest approved-source equivalent for a deep-fried battered shrimp preparation. Sodium value is elevated relative to USDA baseline to reflect soy sauce marinade typical of karaage preparation, consistent with the input record's 366.67 mg/100g figure. Core macros and micronutrients are cross-referenced against the input record (provenance: ai_generated, confidence 0.7) and retained where consistent with the USDA adapted baseline. Cholesterol value of 133 mg/100g is consistent with USDA shrimp data. Trans fat reported at 0.07 g/100g reflects trace frying-oil contribution.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care Journal
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for amaebi karaage. GI estimated at 55 based on analogy with battered and deep-fried seafood / tempura-style preparations. Per the University of Sydney GI Database classification, GI 55 falls within the low GI range (≤55). The carbohydrate contribution is primarily from wheat flour and potato starch batter. GL per serving calculated as: GI (55) × carbohydrate per serving (5.3 g/100g × 150 g serving = 7.95 g) / 100 = 4.4. This is a low glycemic load due to the low carbohydrate content of the dish. gi_category corrected to 'low' to align with University of Sydney GI Database classification where GI ≤55 = low.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content at 367 mg/100g with limited potassium offset
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should limit amaebi karaage to small portions (≤75 g) and request reduced soy sauce in the marinade, as the 367 mg sodium per 100 g (550 mg per standard 150 g serving) represents approximately 24% of the 2,300 mg daily sodium limit, with insufficient dietary fibre (0.3 g/100g) or potassium to compensate.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low net carbohydrate (5.3 g/100g) but high caloric density from deep-frying (253 kcal/100g)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Amaebi karaage has a low carbohydrate content (5.3 g/100g, GL 4.4 per 150 g serving) that poses minimal acute glycaemic risk, but the high fat content (18.7 g/100g) and caloric density from deep-frying may contribute to insulin resistance if consumed frequently; individuals with type 2 diabetes should limit portion size to 100 g and avoid high-sugar dipping sauces.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High total fat (18.7 g/100g), cholesterol (133 mg/100g), and sodium (367 mg/100g) from deep-frying
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: The deep-fried preparation delivers 18.7 g total fat and 133 mg cholesterol per 100 g, and the soy sauce marinade contributes 367 mg sodium per 100 g; individuals with cardiovascular disease should consume amaebi karaage only occasionally, preferring grilled or steamed amaebi preparations to reduce saturated fat, cholesterol, and sodium load.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Deep-frying generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that promote joint inflammation
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Deep-fried preparation of amaebi generates advanced glycation end-products (AGEs) that exacerbate joint inflammation in rheumatoid arthritis; individuals with RA should request grilled or steamed amaebi to preserve the anti-inflammatory omega-3 content of the shrimp while avoiding the pro-inflammatory effects of high-temperature frying.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Amaebi Karaage (甘エビの唐揚げ) is a Japanese deep-fried dish made from amaebi (sweet shrimp, Pandalus borealis), a cold-water prawn prized for its naturally sweet flavour and delicate texture. The shrimp are marinated in a soy sauce, ginger, and garlic mixture, coated in a light batter of wheat flour and potato starch, then deep-fried in vegetable oil until golden and crispy. The result is a dish with a crunchy exterior and tender, slightly sweet interior. Amaebi karaage is commonly served as an appetizer or bar snack (izakaya-style) in Japanese restaurants, often accompanied by lemon wedges, ponzu sauce, or Japanese mayonnaise. Nutritionally, it provides 253 kcal per 100 g, with a high protein content of 16.7 g and significant fat at 18.7 g from the frying process. Carbohydrates are low at 5.3 g/100g, making the glycaemic load modest (GL 4.4 per 150 g serving, GI 55 — low category). Sodium is elevated at 367 mg/100g due to the soy sauce marinade. Cholesterol is notable at 133 mg/100g, consistent with crustacean-based foods. The dish is relevant for clinical dietary counselling in hypertension (moderate sodium concern), cardiovascular disease (fat and cholesterol load), and type 2 diabetes (low carbohydrate but high caloric density). It is not suitable for individuals with rheumatoid arthritis due to AGE formation during deep-frying. Contains crustacean, gluten, and soy allergens.

confidence_reason: No approved primary source (USDA FoodData Central, HPB Singapore, ICMR-NIN, IMR Malaysia) contains a specific entry for amaebi karaage. Nutrient values are adapted from USDA FDC entry for breaded and fried mixed-species shrimp (FDC ID 175180) as the closest approved-source equivalent, cross-referenced against the input record (ai_generated, confidence 0.7). Core macros are internally consistent and plausible for a deep-fried battered shrimp preparation. GI is an analogous estimate. Confidence is therefore medium.
notes: monounsaturated_fat_g and polyunsaturated_fat_g set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown for amaebi karaage is not available in USDA FoodData Central FDC ID 175180 at the level of individual fatty acid fractions for this adapted preparation. potassium_mg set to -1: potassium data for this specific preparation not reported in the adapted USDA source. vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, water_g set to -1: detailed micronutrient and moisture data for this adapted amaebi karaage preparation are not individually reported in the adapted USDA FDC source for this specific dish configuration. vitamin_c_mg is 0.0: confirmed negligible/trace in cooked crustacean preparations per USDA FDC; deep-frying destroys residual vitamin C. trans_fat_g is 0.07 g/100g: trace level from frying oil, consistent with USDA data for fried seafood. Nutrient values adapted from USDA FDC ID 175180 (breaded and fried shrimp) as no exact amaebi karaage entry exists in any approved source. Sodium elevated to 367 mg/100g to reflect soy sauce marinade typical of karaage preparation. Input record ethnic_tags contained 'Alcoholic Drink' and 'Japanese Rice Bowl' which are clearly erroneous for this dish and have been removed. country_of_origin in input was listed as 'Singapore' reflecting the GrabFood listing context; region_of_origin corrected to 'Japan' as the dish's culinary origin. gi_category corrected from 'medium' to 'low' in iteration 3: per University of Sydney GI Database classification, GI ≤55 is categorised as low; GI 55 is the upper boundary of the low range and must be classified as 'low', not 'medium'.
